title: for loop
successMessage: Three parts: init (int i=1), condition (i<=5), update (i++). i++ increments after each body execution. For step 2: use i += 2 in the update slot.

title: Range-based for — iterate containers
successMessage: const auto& — const means read-only, & means reference (no copy). Without &, each element is copied on every iteration. For large objects, always use references.

title: while loop
instruction:
`while` loops while the condition is true. Use when you don't know the iteration count upfront.

⚠️ Always ensure the condition eventually becomes false — infinite loops freeze programs.
successMessage: 11² = 121 > 100. The break exits the loop immediately. while + break is the pattern for 'search until found'. The countdown demonstrates while for decreasing sequences.

title: do-while — run at least once
instruction:
`do-while` executes the body first, then checks the condition — guaranteed one run.

Ideal for: menus (show options then check choice), input validation (prompt then validate).
successMessage: Powers of 2: 1 2 4 8 16 32 64 — stops when n*2 would exceed 64. 1+2+...+10 = 55 (the famous Gauss sum). do-while runs at least once; the sum of 1-10 needs all 10 iterations.

title: break and continue
instruction:
- `break` — exit the loop immediately
- `continue` — skip the rest of this iteration, go to the next

Find the first number divisible by both 7 and 11, then skip multiples of 3 in a range.
successMessage: 77 = 7 × 11. break exits as soon as it's found. continue skips 3, 6, 9... without an else branch. Clean and readable.

title: Nested loops
instruction:
Loops inside loops. The inner loop runs completely for every outer iteration.

Print a 5×5 multiplication table.
hint: for (int i=1; i<=5; i++) { for (int j=1; j<=5; j++) { cout << setw(4) << i*j; } cout << endl; }
successMessage: setw(4) right-aligns in 4 characters. Nested loops power matrices, 2D grids, and pair comparisons. Row i, Column j — that's every 2D data structure.

title: FizzBuzz
instruction:
The classic programming interview test — print 1 to 30:
- Multiple of 3: `Fizz`
- Multiple of 5: `Buzz`
- Multiple of both: `FizzBuzz`

Check for 15 first, or it never triggers.
successMessage: % 15 before % 3 and % 5 — if you check % 3 first, 15 prints 'Fizz' instead of 'FizzBuzz'. Ordering conditions correctly is a core programming skill. Chapter 6 done!
